Source: [here](https://mravinderreddy.substack.com/p/the-two-roads-out-of-the-arc).

The Two Roads Out of the Arc

# The Two Roads Out of the Arc

### One highland crescent seeded both Europe and India, through two different peoples who headed off in opposite directions. Call it the ZAA arc: Zagros, Armenia, Anatolia.

[](https://substack.com/@mravinderreddy)

![Ravinder Reddy's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2Tvm!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F77da9531-28fb-41f8-b757-599e5b73f4c6_1202x1204.png)

[Ravinder Reddy](https://substack.com/@mravinderreddy)

Jun 07, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nK7l!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F132c351d-c19e-486d-9291-7a3310023e44_1672x941.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nK7l!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F132c351d-c19e-486d-9291-7a3310023e44_1672x941.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Around 8,000 BCE, two groups of farmers lived at opposite ends of the same mountain crescent and had practically nothing to do with each other. One sat in the hills of central Anatolia. The other lived over 1,000 km east, in the foothills of the Zagros. They grew wheat and barley, and they also herded goats and sheep, but genetically they were about as far apart as a Norwegian is from a Han Chinese.

When David Reich’s lab sequenced the first farmers of the Near East in 2016, the data backed that up. The early farmers of Anatolia and Zagros were, in the team’s words, strongly differentiated, each descended from its own local hunter-gatherers. Reich’s lab summarizes it by saying something like this: these populations were as different from one another as present-day Europeans are from East Asians.

So it’s fair to say the crescent held two separate beginnings, and they had their own paths. The western one ran into Europe. The eastern one ran into India. That crescent, Anatolia through Armenia into the Zagros, ended up behind both Europe and India. Same source, two roads, and they would not meet again for thousands of years.

## Strangers at either end

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The northern rim of the Fertile Crescent runs in a long arc of hill country, a few hundred kilometers wide, curving from western Anatolia around to the Zagros. Farming got invented along it more or less all at once. The people doing the inventing were not one population spreading one idea. They were locals, valley by valley, taming whatever grew and grazed in front of them. And some of those valleys were a very long way apart.

At the western tip of the arc sat Anatolian farmers, the people of Çatalhöyük with its bull horns and plastered skulls. At the eastern tip, in the Zagros, sat a separate farming people around sites like Ganj Dareh, where the goats were penned between the houses and the dead were buried under the floors. Same tools, same animals, two different worlds, and a thousand years of living a few hundred kilometers apart without blending in any meaningful way.

The difference went deeper than farming and crops. Both poles carried a deep streak of the same ancient lineage, one that geneticists and researchers call Basal Eurasian. In the Zagros it made up close to half their ancestry; in Anatolia it was less, but still a large share. It branched off near the very start of the human journey outside Africa, roughly 50,000 years ago, before the other non-African populations had even separated from one another. It also stayed clear of most of the Neanderthal interbreeding that swept the rest of that founding population around the same time, which is why it carried almost no Neanderthal DNA. So the oldest layer of ancestry in this story is a southern one, and it sits right here in the arc. Tens of thousands of years later, when the steppe finally had its turn around 4,000 BCE, it was building on a base that was already there.

Two peoples, then, sharing a crescent, a crop list, and one deep root far in the past, but already pulled apart on everything since. From here their paths fork, and stay forked for thousands of years.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jBCX!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe27d64ea-74f7-4ce9-a693-55b0d60700d3_1180x600.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jBCX!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe27d64ea-74f7-4ce9-a693-55b0d60700d3_1180x600.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The two farmer poles of the arc. Anatolia at one end, the Zagros at the other, as genetically far apart as Europeans and East Asians today. Lazaridis et al., Nature 2016.

## The western road

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Around 6,700 BCE the Anatolian farmers ran out of room, and they started moving.

They went two ways at once. One stream took to the water, moving across the Aegean island by island, Crete to the Cyclades to the Greek mainland, carrying seed grain and live animals in open boats across the short blue gaps. The other stream went overland, up out of Greece and into the Balkans along the river valleys, following the Vardar and the Struma into the heart of the continent. By 6,000 BCE the wheat had reached the Carpathian basin on the middle Danube.

For decades nobody could say whether these farmers were newcomers or just local foragers who had learned to plant. Ancient DNA settled it much later. The farmers who turned up in Europe were a different people, freshly arrived, whose closest genetic match was Neolithic Anatolia. They came from the arc.

Europe’s hunter-gatherers had held the continent for fourteen thousand years, and they had dark skin and pale blue eyes, a combination that has nearly vanished today. The men from La Braña in Spain and Loschbour in Luxembourg carried exactly that look. The incoming farmers were close to the reverse: they carried the strongest light-skin variant in West Eurasia at about 99 percent, and tended toward darker eyes. The modern European face did not exist yet. It had to be mixed, slowly, out of these two strangers meeting along the frontier.

They had one thing in common with the people they replaced. Neither could drink milk. The gene that lets adults digest fresh milk was rare in farmers and foragers alike, and it stayed rare for thousands of years after the first cows were milked, rising to high frequency only in the Bronze and Iron Ages. The herders came first; the milk drinkers came much later.

The frontier did not move smoothly. North of the Danube the soil turns heavy and cold and wet, nothing like the light Mediterranean ground the Anatolian toolkit was built for, and for a thousand years the farming wave simply stopped, pinned against the edge of the Hungarian plain. Then around 5,500 BCE a new culture cracked the heavy northern soil, and the frontier surged again, all the way to the Atlantic and the North Sea.

When the dust settled, Europe had been rebuilt from the southeast. The western pole of the arc was now the bedrock of the continent.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XoCm!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fff9ccf69-9be6-47fa-9172-31eca34b6e93_2158x1373.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XoCm!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fff9ccf69-9be6-47fa-9172-31eca34b6e93_2158x1373.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Farming walked out of the arc into Europe, across the Aegean and up the Balkan rivers, stalling at the Danube. Map: Gronenborn, Horejs, Börner and Ober 2023

That bedrock did not hold. Over the millennia that followed, the first-farmer world of Europe came apart with real violence. Mass graves from the period hold dozens of people struck from behind and buried without their heads. At a cave in northern Spain, one farming group butchered, cooked, and ate at least eleven of their neighbors in a single episode around 3700 BCE, and the data shows the victims were locals, not strangers. Plague was moving through the same villages at the same time. The people who rebuilt Europe were themselves nearly erased before a new population arrived from the east. How that collapse happened, and how much of it was warfare, hunger, and disease, is a story for its own piece, and I will come back to it in the future.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xWav!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd961f7d3-371e-441d-bcee-0f94c6db1c20_1584x1222.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xWav!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd961f7d3-371e-441d-bcee-0f94c6db1c20_1584x1222.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## The eastern road

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Zagros pole never went to Europe. It turned the other way.

From the foothills of western Iran, this lineage spread out across the Iranian plateau and pushed east, into the oasis country of Central Asia and eventually to the doorstep of the Indus. The great Bronze Age civilization of Central Asia, BMAC, sits on this road like a hinge, the turning point where the eastern arm of the arc swings down toward the subcontinent.

By the time it reached the edge of the Indus Valley, this Iranian-related ancestry had mixed with a deep, indigenous South Asian lineage that we call AASI, the oldest human stratum in India. The blend shows up across the borderlands of the Indus world, in the genomes from sites like Shahr-i-Sokhta and in the individual recovered from the Harappan cemetery at Rakhigarhi. That mixture, Iranian-related plus indigenous AASI, became the western pole of South Asian ancestry, and it is still there. The two biggest modern surveys, a study of more than 2,700 whole genomes in 2024 and the national GenomeIndia atlas in 2026, both find Iranian-farmer ancestry as the largest single western component across the subcontinent, from Punjab to Tamil Nadu.

The eastern road carries a twist the western one does not. The Iranian-related ancestry in South Asia broke away from the Iranian plateau lineage very early, before anyone in the region had planted a seed, before 8,000 BCE. I must admit that timing is awkward, and the field has not fully closed the case on it. If the ancestry runs deeper than farming, did it really spread with farmers at all, or with some earlier movement of hunter-gatherers we are only starting to see? In recent years, Narasimhan and the Reich group lean toward a farming model; some others read it as older. The one thing nobody disputes is that this western pole of India is not steppe, and it is not Anatolian. It was moving east, in whatever form, while the Anatolian farmers were still loading their boats.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0UUa!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc0d66d28-f737-4f23-83c4-eaaece42c7c6_1280x591.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0UUa!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc0d66d28-f737-4f23-83c4-eaaece42c7c6_1280x591.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The steppe boom went both ways. Yamnaya-derived ancestry flooded into Europe by around 2500 BCE, then reached South Asia much later and lighter, after 2000 BCE. The path into India is still debated. Narasimhan et al., Science 2019.

The eastern pole turns up almost everywhere researchers look. Sequence a West Eurasian population from 9,000 BCE onward and a Zagros related component is usually somewhere in the mix. The Anatolian farmers have some. So do the Caucasus foragers, the steppe pastoralists, the people of the Indus, and living Iranians, Kurds, Baloch, Gujaratis, and Tamils. For a lineage that started in one stretch of foothills, it got around quite a bit.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!81Hg!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F80bb9d99-b9bb-4334-aaec-8b00ec82c45f_960x1370.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!81Hg!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F80bb9d99-b9bb-4334-aaec-8b00ec82c45f_960x1370.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The individual from Rakhigarhi, in the Indus world. Iranian-related plus indigenous ancestry, and no steppe. Shinde et al., Cell 2019.

## Where the two roads meet

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For thousands of years the two poles ran on separate tracks. The Anatolian side built Europe. The Iranian side built the foundation of India. They did not meet in the arc, and they did not meet in Europe or in India. They met on the grassland in between.

North of the Black and Caspian Seas, the Caucasus hunter-gatherers, who are near-twins of the Iranian pole, drifted up onto the Pontic-Caspian steppe and mixed with the Eastern European foragers already living there. The result of that mixture is the population everyone has heard of (especially if you are chronically online on X): the Yamnaya, the wagon-driving kurgan-building pastoralists of the steppe. By the Reich lab’s own decomposition the Yamnaya are roughly 40% Caucasus ancestry and 60% Eastern European forager. The internet famous steppe people are already close to half southern by the time they exist, around 4,000 BCE. Nearly half their ancestry came up from the Caucasus, the same highland south that produced the Iranian pole.

So the grassland is not a third source standing apart from the arc. It is the place the two roads crossed AGAIN, after a long detour, and produced something new. The steppe sits downstream of the crescent, not upstream of it.

Then the grassland boomed. Around 3,500 BCE, the Yamnaya expanded off the steppe in every direction, one of the largest and fastest population movements in European prehistory, pushing a fresh layer of steppe ancestry west into Europe and, more than a thousand years later, south through BMAC toward the Indus. Both halves of the arc’s scattered family got a second visit, this time from their reunited cousins riding wagons. So in both cases, Europe and India, the steppe was delivering a recombined version of ancestry the arc had supplied in the first place.

## Recap

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

So let’s summarize what we learnt so far: A single bow of highland country invented farming. Its western tip moved into Europe and became the continent’s first farmers, the ancestry almost every modern European still carries. Its eastern tip flowed into Iran and on to the Indus and became the western half of nearly every South Asian. The two lines did not touch again until their descendants met on the steppe and braided together into the peoples of the Bronze Age.

So in many ways Europe and India are cousins, in the literal genetic sense, raised at opposite ends of the same crescent and sent off in opposite directions. Most of the deep map of who we are keeps running back to that one stretch of mountain country between Anatolia and the Zagros.

It comes down to two groups of farmers, nine thousand years ago, living at opposite ends of the arc. They never met. They spoke different languages and they didn’t look alike. But between them they carried the ancestry that would one day reach from Europe all the way to South Asia.

The genetics keeps pointing to the same thing. There was no single homeland that fanned out in every direction. There was an arc, and it ran two ways: west into Europe and east into India.

Call it ZAA. It is where both roads began.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qD3t!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa5610658-7a3e-43fe-b51f-fc34e5393e3f_2376x1836.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qD3t!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa5610658-7a3e-43fe-b51f-fc34e5393e3f_2376x1836.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Sources

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

- Lazaridis et al. (2016), “Genomic insights into the origin of farming
  in the ancient Near East,” Nature 536:419 to 424. The Anatolian and
  Zagros farmer poles strongly differentiated, each from local
  hunter-gatherers, as different from one another as present-day
  Europeans are from East Asians; the Zagros pole close to half Basal
  Eurasian, the Anatolian pole somewhat less.

- Haak et al. (2015) and Mathieson et al. (2015, Nature): early European
  farmers as a distinct Anatolian-derived population; pigmentation
  (SLC24A5) and the dark-skinned, pale-eyed hunter-gatherers.

- Gronenborn, Horejs, Börner and Ober (2023): mapped chronology of the
  farming expansion across Europe, the Aegean and Balkan routes, the
  Danube stall.

- Narasimhan et al. (2019, Science) and Shinde et al. (2019, Cell): the
  Indus Periphery cline, Iranian-related plus AASI ancestry, with the
  Iranian component diverging before 8,000 BCE; steppe ancestry reaching
  South Asia only after about 2000 BCE.

- Kerdoncuff et al. (2024) and GenomeIndia (2026): Iranian-farmer
  ancestry as the largest western component across modern South Asia.

- Lazaridis et al. (2022), “The genetic history of the Southern Arc,”
  Science: Yamnaya formed roughly forty percent Caucasus hunter-gatherer
  and sixty percent Eastern European hunter-gatherer.

- Curry (2025), Science: organized violence among Europe’s early
  farmers, including headless mass graves, and the late-Neolithic
  population collapse that preceded the steppe arrival.

- Saladié et al. (2025), “Evidence of neolithic cannibalism among
  farming communities at El Mirador cave, Sierra de Atapuerca, Spain,”
  Scientific Reports: at least eleven local individuals butchered and
  consumed in a single episode around 3700 BCE.
